---
title: "Recruitment"
subtitle: Finding Future Leaders
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Recruiting and employing our students and graduates {#recruitment}
Many of our students in Computer Science are recruited _before_ they graduate by working for an employer on a fixed-term contract, either part-time, full-time and as summer interns or for a year long placement with [industrial experience](experience.html). This page describes some of the options for recruiting and employing students from the Department of Computer Science, as well as those studying other degree programs at the University of Manchester.

![Are these graduates the future of your organisation? If you want them to be, you need to engage early and often with students *before* they graduate. This will increase your chances of recruiting them. The picture shows University of Manchester graduates celebrating their graduation outside the [Samuel Alexander](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Samuel_Alexander) building with spontaneous photobombing by [Gavin Brown](https://research.manchester.ac.uk/en/persons/gavin.brown)](employing-your-future.png)


## About our students {#students}

We are one of the biggest departments of Computer Science in the [Russell Group](https://www.russellgroup.ac.uk/) of Universities and home to large community of active students. As of 2026 we have *over 1000 undergraduate students* studying one of [two main Bachelors degrees](https://www.cs.manchester.ac.uk/study/undergraduate/courses/):

1. BSc (Hons) Computer Science 
1. BSc (Hons) Computer Science with Mathematics

Both degree programmes are available with [Industrial Experience](experience.html) (IE), a year in industry. Alongside our undergraduate community we currently have:

* Over 240 postgraduate taught students, doing Masters degrees
* Over 240 PhD students doing research supervised by academics in the Department

As of 2026, the [entry tariff](https://www.ucas.com/ucas/tariff-calculator) for our undergraduate students is **A* A* A ** including an **A* ** in Mathematics, and a minimum of *one* science subject from Computer Science, Further Mathematics, Biology, Chemistry and Physics or [equivalent qualifications](https://www.manchester.ac.uk/study/undergraduate/courses/2026/00560/bsc-computer-science/) from outside the UK. We typically have around ~300 undergraduate students graduating annually, alongside around ~240 Masters and ~70 PhD students graduating every year.

# Recruiting students from the University of Manchester {#recruiting}

There's at least three routes for recruiting students at the University of Manchester:

* From specific degree programmes like Computer Science via the Wednesday Waggle
* From any degree programmes e.g. STEM or other degrees via various routes
* From [events](events.html) and [careers fairs](fairs.html) organised by the Department of Computer Science or Careers service

These are described in more detail below:

## The Wednesday Waggle for Computing {#waggle}
With a student population of over 40,000 students enrolled on over 1,000 different degree programmes, it is very easy for opportunities from employers to get lost in the blizzard of information overload on teaching, [student events](#studentsoc) and corporate communication from the University. To help our students navigate this space, we highlight events and vacancies in a tailored weekly jobs newsletter for Computer Science, called the *Wednesday Waggle* see [waggle.cs.manchester.ac.uk](https://waggle.cs.manchester.ac.uk/) shown in @fig-waggle

![The Wednesday Waggle summarises key opportunities for students in Computer Science. The name of the newsletter comes from Bees, a Mancunian symbol of community and work ethic since the industrial revolution. [Waggle dance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Waggle_dance) artwork by [Visual Thinkery](https://visualthinkery.com) is licensed under CC-BY-ND. 🐝](wednesday-triple.png){#fig-waggle} 

This email newsletter goes out to 1000+ Bachelors and 240+ Masters students in Computer Science each week on Wednesdays during term-time. The Wednesday Waggle does not replace the [all of sources that students can use to find career opportunities](https://www.cdyf.me/finding), but summarises key vacancies and events in a handy weekly digest for busy Computer Scientists. If you would like us to highlight something in the Wednesday Waggle for Computer Scientists, contact the editor of the newsletter, [Duncan Hull](contact.html).

## Recruiting from other degree programmes {#beyondcs}
If you're recruiting beyond Computer Science, we recommend you contact: 

* The central careers service which serves the other ~39,000 students at the University at [www.careers.manchester.ac.uk](https://www.careers.manchester.ac.uk)
* Academic employability leads in the relevant Department e.g. [Physics](https://www.physics.manchester.ac.uk/), [Maths](https://www.maths.manchester.ac.uk/), [Chemistry](https://www.chemistry.manchester.ac.uk/), Mechanical, Aerospace & Civil Engineering ([MACE](https://www.mace.manchester.ac.uk/)), [Materials Science](https://www.materials.manchester.ac.uk/) and Electrical & Electronic Engineering ([EEE](https://www.eee.manchester.ac.uk/)) etc

Employers can advertise vacancies and events across the whole University for free via `careerconnect.manchester.ac.uk`, for details see [employers.manchester.ac.uk](https://www.employers.manchester.ac.uk) 

Each department has an employability lead with responsibility for liaising with industrial partners relevant to their students degree programs. As an academic, the employability will have direct access to those students through their teaching and research. Contact the relevant Department for details.

# Employing students before they graduate {#employcs}

There are several models for of employing students before they graduate. These include part-time, full-time, term-time, summer-time or as a year long placement students during a “sandwich” year in industry.

![](PASS-gallery-large.jpg) 

## Industrial Experience Year {#withie}

Around a third of our undergraduate students are successful in securing an industrial experience (IE) year in industry before graduation, see [industrial experience](experience.html)


## Summer internships for Computer Scientists {#summer}
As well as year long placements, a significant proportion of our students are employed on full-time contracts over a summer for anything between 4-12 weeks, starting early June and finishing early September. As summer internships are not part of a specific degree program, sthey do not need to be approved by the University.

Many of our students do summer internships between their first and second, or second and final year of study. We encourage all our students to take skilled work during summer if they can and highlight key internships for students in the [Wednesday Waggle](https://waggle.cs.manchester.ac.uk/) and elsewhere.

## Part-time work for Computer Scientists {#parttime}
Some students choose to work part-time during term-time for an hourly rate of pay, provided they can balance their study and employment commitments. Casual or [zero-hours contracts](https://www.gov.uk/contract-types-and-employer-responsibilities/zero-hour-contracts) are one way to facilitate this, provided that employers realise that during term-time, a students top priority should be study, not employment. 

An example of part-time employment on casual contracts is Imago, our student software company [imago.cs.manchester.ac.uk](https://imago.cs.manchester.ac.uk/). Other employers have employed our students part-time on similar contracts as well.


## Engaging directly with students through student societies {#studentsoc}

The University of Manchester Students Union (UMSU) is the largest in the UK with over 500 student societies. Many of these societies have a scientific, technical, engineering or mathematical interests including. A small sample is shown, in no particular order in @tbl-umsu


| Student society  | Link   |  
|:---|:---|
|  Startups  | [accelerateme.co](https://accelerateme.co) |
| UNIversity Computer Science | [unicsmcr.com](https://unicsmcr.com/) |
| Manchester University Data Science & Artificial Intelligence | [mudsai.com](https://www.mudsai.com) |
| IBM Z society | [IBMZ.uom](https://linktr.ee/ibmz.uom) |
| Maths Society | [MathSoc](https://manchesterstudentsunion.com/activities/view/mathsoc) |
| Manchester Intelligence Society (Cybersecurity) | [MIS](https://manchesterstudentsunion.com/activities/view/manchester-intelligence) |
| Robosoc | [uom-robosoc.com](https://uom-robosoc.com) |
| Manchester Trading and Investment Society | [MUTIS](https://manchesterstudentsunion.com/activities/view/mutis) |
| Hyperloop Manchester | [hyperloopmanchester.co.uk](https://www.hyperloopmanchester.co.uk) |
| Makerspace Manchester | [makerspacemanchester.org](https://www.makerspacemanchester.org) |
| Manchester Stinger Motorports | [formulastudentuom.com](https://formulastudentuom.com) |
| Google Developers Group (GDG) Manchester | [GDGManchester](https://linktr.ee/gdgmanchester) |

: A small selection of societies at the Unviversity of Manchester with scientific, technical, engineering and mathematical interests {#tbl-umsu}


One way to engage with students is to sponsor and support their events by contacting relevant societies directly. This includes sponsoring hackathons and offering speakers for talks, panels, workshops, insights and other events. 

![Representing over 40,000 students and with over 500 student societies, the [University of Manchester Students' Union](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Manchester_Students%27_Union) (UMSU) is the largest in the UK, see [manchesterstudentsunion.com](https://manchesterstudentsunion.com)](umsu-panorama.png) 


# Employing Computer Science graduates {#graduates}

Finally, there is graduate employment, either through graduate schemes or a graduate jobs. [@graduatescheme] The Higher Education Statistics Agency (HESA) collects data from UK graduates 15 months after they have graduated via the annual graduate outcomes survey. [@graduateoutcomes] Key data from this national survey is summarised in @tbl-gradoutcomes. According to this data, the average Computer Science graduate salary 15 months after graduation from the University of Manchester is **£40k**, although there are significant variations by sector and location.


| Degree  | Students in work or study   |  Students in highly skilled employment  | Salary after 15 months  |  
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
|  BSc Computer Science, 3 year, University of Manchester [@BScCS3] | 85% ($n$=40)  | 75% ($n$=30)  | £40k ($n$=45)  |   
|  BSc Computer Science [with IE](experience.html), 4 year, University of Manchester [@BScCS4] |  91% ($n$=75)  | 85% ($n$=60)  | £40k ($n$=45)  |   
| BSc Computer Science, University of Anywhere, UK | `?` | `?` | £32k ($n$=6420) |
| Bachelors degree, University of Anywhere, UK | 88%  ($n$=358,045) [@graduateoutcomes] | `?` | £28.5k ($n$=358,045)  |

:A summary of graduate outcomes data in Computer Science at the University of Manchester. Graduate employability data via `discoveruni.gov.uk` where $n$ is the number of students who answered question via the graduate outcomes survey. Click on link in degree column to see original data.  Data is based on students graduating in 2022, who were surveyed during 2023/4 with HESA publishing this data in July 2025. 🎓 {#tbl-gradoutcomes}
